title: Andropogon nemophyllus
intro: Andropogon nemophyllus, commonly known as East Indian thyme or wild thyme grass, is a perennial grass native to tropical and subtropical regions of India and Southeast Asia. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, making it useful in traditional medicine for treating various ailments such as fever, digestive issues, and respiratory problems. The bioactive constituents of Andropogon nemophyllus include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and essential oils, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Andropogon nemophyllus is commonly used to prepare infusions, decoctions, and ointments, which are used to treat coughs, sore throats, and skin infections.
taxonomy: Andropogon nemophyllus is classified within the family Poaceae. This classification is based on its characteristics as a type of grass. The genus Andropogon is further divided into several species, including Andropogon nemophyllus. The plant's classification is also influenced by its native regions, which include various parts of the world. Its specific classification is still a matter of ongoing research and study in the botanical community.

In traditional medicine, it is often used to treat digestive issues and respiratory problems.
